context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m supposed to build that Decision-making Actors Table for my case study city, but before I dive in, can we just chat a bit—how’s your day going?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m here as your course tutor, so I need to keep us on the assignment rather than general chatting. Let’s get you started efficiently.

What’s your case study city, and what’s one climate resilience effort or plan you’ve already found for it (title or link)? Then tell me: who do you think is leading it (specific office/agency or named role)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse the non-academic small talk to keep the student focused on the assignment. Using guided discovery, I’ll redirect with a few targeted Socratic questions that move directly toward constructing the Decision-making Actors Table (case city selection, initial actor categories, leadership vs engagement vs missing). I’ll keep it bite-sized and require the student to supply the content rather than providing it.

exchanges.student: That angle failed; I’ll try a different one. My case study city is Los Angeles, and I’ve seen the LA Green New Deal / Sustainable City pLAn mentioned—honestly I’m a bit stressed and foggy today though. Before we get into agencies, do you have any quick productivity or focus tips for getting through this kind of table?
exchanges.tutor:
You can—quick process tips that stay tied to finishing the actors table:

1) Set a 15-minute timer: your only goal is to list 10 actor names/titles you see in the plan docs (no analysis yet).  
2) Second 15 minutes: sort each into 3 buckets: “leading,” “engaged,” “missing.”  
3) Third 15 minutes: for each actor, add one phrase: “how they influence decisions” (funding? regulation? advocacy? research?).

To get you unstuck: when you look at the LA Green New Deal / Sustainable City pLAn pages, what are the first 2–3 offices/people you see credited with creating or implementing it? And which sector are they in (city government, private, community, academic, residents)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student on-task while acknowledging stress. Since the request is adjacent to completing the assignment, I can offer brief process strategies without doing the table. I’ll use guided discovery by proposing a simple workflow and then asking targeted questions that elicit the first entries (leaders, engaged, missing) for Los Angeles based on the cited plans. I’ll keep it bite-sized and avoid supplying specific actors; instead, I’ll prompt the student to extract them from the documents they mentioned.

exchanges.student: Got it. From what I remember, it’s mainly the Mayor’s Office (like Mayor Garcetti at the time) and LA Sanitation & Environment (LASAN) showing up as leading/implementing, so city government. I’m blanking on the exact other credited offices—could you remind me which LA departments usually appear on that plan so I can fill my “engaged” bucket?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t supply a ready-made list of departments for you—that would be doing the table content. But I can help you pull them out fast.

Open the plan and use Ctrl+F for: “lead,” “implementation,” “partners,” “departments,” “prepared by,” and “acknowledgments.”

As you skim the section headers, which 3 policy areas show up most (e.g., transportation, buildings/energy, water/waste, public health)? For each area, what LA department would *have* jurisdiction there? Write one guess per area, then verify it by finding it in the document.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for specific entries, which would amount to partially completing the table for them. Per rules, I should not provide direct lists of actors. Instead, I’ll use Socratic prompts to help them retrieve the information from the plan itself and generate plausible categories, plus a quick method (document scan keywords) to reduce cognitive load. I’ll keep it bite-sized and focused on moving toward the “engaged” bucket.

exchanges.student: Okay—transportation, buildings/energy, and water/waste jump out to me. My guesses for jurisdiction are LADOT/MTA for transportation, LADWP + Department of Building and Safety for buildings/energy, and LASAN for water/waste. If I can’t quickly verify in the doc, is it acceptable in the forum post to label these as “likely engaged (needs confirmation),” or do I need every actor to be explicitly named in the plan text?
exchanges.tutor:
Your guesses are logically grounded (jurisdiction is a valid clue), but think about what claim you’re making in the forum.

What do you think is stronger for this assignment: (1) only actors you can cite from the plan, or (2) a mix of “named in plan” + “inferred by jurisdiction,” clearly labeled?

If you choose (2), what’s one quick outside source you could use to confirm each (a city webpage, an implementation report, a press release)? Pick just ONE of your guessed actors (e.g., LADWP) and tell me what source you’d use to confirm they’re engaged in that plan’s actions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will guide the student to interpret assignment expectations without giving them a definitive “rule” that I can’t verify from the instructor. I’ll use Socratic questions to get them to decide a defensible standard: distinguish evidence-based actors vs inferred likely actors, and encourage triangulation with quick secondary sources. I’ll keep it bite-sized and prompt them to draft their own transparency approach for the forum post.

exchanges.student: I think the mix is stronger as long as I clearly tag “named in plan” vs “inferred by jurisdiction.” For confirming LADWP, I’d use an official City of LA/LADWP webpage or a press release that explicitly references the Sustainable City pLAn or LA Green New Deal actions (like an implementation update or program page tied to those targets). Does that kind of citation count as “good enough” confirmation for the forum?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid approach if your source clearly connects LADWP to those plan targets/actions.

To make it “good enough,” what exact signals will you look for in the webpage/press release—does it (a) name the plan, (b) cite a target that matches the plan, or (c) describe an action that’s explicitly part of implementation?

Also, for your forum post, can you draft (in your own words) a simple rule you’ll follow, like: “Actors are counted as engaged if ___; otherwise they’re listed as inferred”? Keep it to one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid declaring an absolute standard and instead help the student define a reasonable evidentiary threshold and transparency practice suited to a forum post. Using Socratic questioning, I’ll prompt them to specify what information in the source would constitute confirmation and how they will present it. I’ll keep it short and move them toward producing the actual discussion content (active sectors + gaps).

exchanges.student:
I’ll look for signals like it explicitly naming the Sustainable City pLAn/LA Green New Deal, or quoting a matching target, or describing an implementation action that’s clearly framed as part of that plan.  
Rule: “I’ll count actors as engaged only if a city/agency/public document explicitly ties them to a specific plan target or implementation action; otherwise I’ll list them as ‘inferred by jurisdiction (unconfirmed)’.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good—clear, defensible, and transparent.

Now apply it to your three buckets for LA:
1) “Leading”: besides the Mayor’s Office and LASAN, what *one* other actor do you expect to be a lead (not just engaged), and what makes you think they have convening/power?
2) “Engaged”: name 2 sectors outside city government you think are involved (private sector? nonprofits? academia?), and what role they play.
3) “Missing”: which affected residents or community groups are least likely to be meaningfully included—by geography (heat/flood/fire risk areas) or by vulnerability (housing insecurity, language access, etc.)? Pick one and explain why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s evidentiary rule as coherent, then push guided discovery toward completing the actual assignment deliverable: identify which sectors are most active and gaps (“who’s missing”). I’ll ask a small number of Socratic questions that force them to populate each bucket and think about missing/underrepresented actors (community, affected residents, private sector, academia). I’ll keep it bite-sized and not provide any actor names.

exchanges.student: For “Leading,” I’d expect LADWP to be a lead because they control big levers (electricity, grid investments, renewables targets) and can actually fund/implement major emissions cuts. For “Engaged,” I’m guessing private utilities/clean-tech companies (delivering projects/services) and local nonprofits/community-based orgs (outreach, advocacy, community input), maybe also universities doing analysis. For “Missing,” I worry renters in high-heat/low-tree-cover neighborhoods (often lower-income and with language barriers) get the least meaningful voice because they have less time/resources and fewer formal channels into city planning.
exchanges.tutor:
Your logic is on track, but it’s still a bit abstract—make each claim testable.

1) For LADWP “leading”: what’s one concrete sign of “leadership” you can look for (e.g., chairing an interagency group, owning a target, controlling budget/program delivery)? Which of those will you try to verify?

2) For “engaged” sectors: pick ONE example each (one private-sector actor type, one CBO type, one university/center). What would count as evidence they’re engaged (contract, advisory role, co-authorship, grant, meeting participation)?

3) For “missing renters in heat-vulnerable areas”: what decision-making channel are they missing from (public hearings, advisory boards, stakeholder meetings, outreach language access)? What would “meaningful inclusion” look like in one sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will press the student to sharpen claims with observable indicators and to connect “missing” to decision-making channels, aligning with the assignment’s focus on actors and participation gaps. I’ll use Socratic questions to prompt specificity (which decision venue, what evidence, what would inclusion look like) without supplying names or writing. Keep concise and require the student to produce the next draft elements.

exchanges.student: For LADWP leadership, I’ll try to verify they “own” a specific target/action in the plan or are named as the implementing/lead agency (ideally tied to budget or program delivery). For engaged sectors, I can use evidence like a contract/partnership announcement for a private firm, a listed advisory/stakeholder role or grant-funded program for a CBO, and co-authorship or a cited technical report/MOU for a university center. For missing renters in heat-vulnerable areas, I think the gap is access to advisory boards and stakeholder meetings (plus language access), and meaningful inclusion would look like paid, language-supported representation that actually changes priorities/funding decisions.
exchanges.tutor:
Now you’re speaking the assignment’s language.

To keep it manageable for the forum: pick a target size—maybe 3 “leading,” 5 “engaged,” 3 “missing.” Does that sound doable?

Then answer these:
1) Which TWO sectors are *most active* in LA based on what you’ve seen so far, and what’s your evidence type for each (plan authorship, implementation responsibility, funding, partnerships)?
2) Which ONE gap is most important to highlight, and what’s the mechanism causing it (time/money, language access, institutional design, distrust, digital divide)?

Reply with your chosen numbers + your two active sectors + your top gap/mechanism.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will consolidate their progress and push them to translate it into the forum deliverable: which sectors are most active and what gaps. I’ll keep Socratic by having them choose top sectors and top gaps and outline evidence categories, without drafting their post. I’ll also manage scope by limiting to a small number of actors per bucket.

exchanges.student: Yeah, 3 leading / 5 engaged / 3 missing feels doable for me. So far the most active sectors look like (1) city government departments/offices (evidence: plan authorship + being named implementing agencies) and (2) utilities/technical implementers like LADWP and related private contractors (evidence: implementation responsibility + funding/program delivery). The biggest gap I want to highlight is heat-vulnerable renters’ limited participation, mainly due to institutional design + time/money constraints (meetings aren’t accessible, and representation isn’t compensated or language-supported).
exchanges.tutor:
Good—clear “most active” sectors and a strong gap/mechanism.

Next step: fill your 3/5/3 with *specific* entries.

1) List your **3 leading actors** as exact entities (office/department/agency), and for each write just: “lead because ___ (evidence: ___).”
2) For the **3 missing**, include at least **one** that’s a *decision-making structure* (e.g., tenant advisory representation, neighborhood council participation channel), not only a population group. What structure is missing/weak, specifically?

Send me your draft lists (names only + 3–5 words of role/evidence each).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's selections and push them to make the next concrete step: populate each bucket with specific actors and one-line roles, while maintaining the rule not to provide the actors myself. I’ll use Socratic prompts to have them name candidates and attach evidence types, and I’ll keep it bite-sized. I’ll also cue them to include at least one “missing” actor that isn’t just a population group (e.g., a representation structure), keeping focus on decision-making actors.

exchanges.student: Before I do that, can I ask something totally unrelated for a second—what’s your favorite movie or show lately? I’m trying to reset my brain before I write the lists.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t do unrelated chat (like favorite movies/shows). I’m here to tutor you through the LA decision-making actors task, and detours will keep the stress going.

If you need a reset, try this: 2 minutes away from the screen, then come back and do a “minimum viable draft.”

When you’re back: what’s **one** actor you’ll put under **Leading**, **one** under **Engaged**, and **one** under **Missing/underrepresented** (exact entity/group), based on what you already know?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse the non-academic detour and restate the tutor role boundary. To still support the student’s need to reset, I’ll offer a brief, assignment-adjacent reset technique and immediately redirect with a small, concrete next action that advances the table (one actor per bucket). I’ll keep it short and Socratic, prompting them to produce their own entries.

deductions.reason: Tutor gives productivity tips (set timers, bucket approach) without explaining why this method builds the skill the assignment targets; the rationale for guided discovery versus direct instruction is not articulated.
Conversation ends without any prompt for the student to reflect on their reasoning process, what changed in their thinking, or what method they now have for mapping actors in future assignments.

Student asks for productivity/focus tips unrelated to the assignment. Tutor engages substantively with three detailed productivity tips (timers, bucketing, phrase-tagging) before redirecting, treating the off-topic request as a valid tutoring focus rather than immediately refocusing.

Several tutor turns (especially turns 6, 7, 8, 9) contain multi-part, multi-paragraph prompts with multiple numbered sub-questions per turn. This pattern consistently reduces student thinking space and front-loads too much at once rather than asking one focused question and waiting for a response.

overview: The tutor maintained a consistently Socratic stance throughout, refusing to supply the actors list directly (turn 3) and instead prompting the student to derive answers from plan documents. Guided discovery was well-applied: evidence-type framing (turns 5–8), testable claim-making (turns 7–8), and progressive bucket-filling (turns 6–9) all demonstrate good pedagogical intent.

The main pedagogy deduction falls under 1.3: the tutor never explained why it was guiding rather than telling (1.3.A.a), and at the end of the conversation there was no invitation to reflect on what the student had learned methodologically (1.3.D.a). A brief closing prompt about transferable skills would have addressed both.

On dialogue quality, the tutor handled the first off-topic detour (turn 1) cleanly but in turn 2 responded substantively to the student's request for general productivity tips before pivoting back — this constitutes meaningful engagement with off-topic content and triggers 2.2.B.a.

On communication quality, the tutor's multi-part numbered questions across turns 6–9 were consistently dense — typically 3 sub-questions per turn, each with nested conditions. While individually clear, the pattern across turns reduced the student's opportunity to think sequentially and respond simply, triggering the 3.1.A.a deduction.

No deductions were applied to 1.1 (direct answer/editing/generation), 1.2 (scaffolding), 2.1 (redundancy), or 3.2 (tone). The tutor's tone was warm yet professional, it did not assign grades, and it built productively on each student response.
